title: Fragment: The Last Grade
description: Found in Room 204 of Maple Heights Elementary after defeating the Arbiter of the Grade. A graded paper, left on the desk. The grade has been crossed out and rewritten several times. The final mark is in a different hand — quieter, less certain, more true.

lines: Room 204 had the quality of a place where a great deal of importance had been assigned to small things for a very long time. The fluorescent light still hummed. The alphabet still ran above the whiteboard in its patient arc.

On the front desk — the teacher's desk, the one facing all the others — a single sheet of paper. Lined, standard, the kind used for in-class work. At the top, a name field left blank. Below it, what appeared to have been an essay, though the words were hard to read under the corrections.

The grade at the top had been written and crossed out. Written again. Crossed out again. Circled, uncircled, written a final time in a hand that was slightly different from the others — smaller, less automatic.

steps.text: Lifting the paper from the desk, the Arbiter's voice arrived — not the voice from the fight, but the one underneath it, the one that had been grading things before there was anyone to grade them for.

lines.speaker: Arbiter of the Grade
lines.text: I graded everything. Every answer, every approach, every silence. I had rubrics for rubrics.

lines.speaker: Arbiter of the Grade
lines.text: I built criteria for what constituted a passing score on having sufficient criteria. I thought that was thoroughness.

lines.speaker: Arbiter of the Grade
lines.text: The problem with a rubric is that it tells you what the answer has to look like before you've seen the answer. That's not evaluation. That's recognition.

lines.speaker: Arbiter of the Grade
lines.text: You showed me something the rubric didn't have a row for.

lines.speaker: Arbiter of the Grade
lines.text: The last grade I gave was the first one I meant.

steps.text: The paper had the weight of something that had been held to the light many times. In the margin — not at the top, not in any of the official grade fields, but in the margin, where no rubric governed — a single mark, written carefully, like a word chosen after a long time of not knowing which word to choose.
The sensation of having been assessed by someone who finally understood that they were also being assessed.

steps.name: Arbiter's Final Paper
steps.description: A test paper where the only mark that matters is written in the margin. Grants +3 ATK. The bearer's first strike in each battle is evaluated honestly. Tooltip: "The last grade I gave was the first one I meant."
